title: Systematic review
body_html:
Systematic review (SR)
A systematic review is a structured and transparent process for identifying, evaluating, and synthesising all available evidence on a clearly defined research question. It is considered the gold standard in the hierarchy of research evidence, especially when accompanied by a meta-analysis.

Key Features
  • Focused clinical question (e.g., using PICO format)
  • Comprehensive and reproducible search strategy
  • Predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria
  • Critical appraisal of study quality
  • Data extraction and synthesis
  • Often registered in PROSPERO and reported using PRISMA guidelines

Benefits
  • Refines and reduces large volumes of literature into concise, actionable evidence
  • Efficient and can prevent unnecessary new studies
  • Enhances generalisability by synthesising across populations and settings
  • Identifies consistency (or inconsistency) between studies
  • Reduces bias and error, increasing reliability
  • Meta-analysis improves power and precision

Meta-Analysis
A meta-analysis is the quantitative component of a systematic review. It combines results from similar studies to produce an overall estimate of effect, often displayed using a forest plot. It can:
  • Resolve uncertainty when individual studies are inconclusive
  • Provide more precise estimates
  • Test for and visualise heterogeneity

Example forest plot. Click on image to enlarge
